content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

GPT Image 2’s offeringThis thought came to me from the ideaplasmic continuum while I was sleepily scrolling from a TikTok on disclosure and astral travel.
I’m here to deliver.

We’ve already seen the pieces for this taking shape.
Onboarding videos from the 90s. Digital Twins for individual humans. Companies posting as their brand. Grok sassing Elon (and others) on X. Chatbot assistants on company websites. Google’s NotebookLM already lets you talk with your documents, ideas, and memories. And in some way, even speaking with an LLM lets you speak with a conglomeration of certain cultural norms, histories, and tendencies, oriented towards your own local field. What if we focused the lens slightly, with intention, to scale this for various needs.
What if founders could chat with their business like they would another person?
What if people in a community could ask it what issues it sees, and what resources they really have?
What if a group of people who are reclaiming their culture and language could encounter it in a new way?
What if institutions could see themselves as living systems, understanding the relationships that sustain them?
What happens when the collective can converse with its own memory? What if it could hear itself (and listen)? What if an organization could develop a relationship with its own memory, identity, and ecology?
This might provide some challenges. For example, the gap between description and behavior, or, ideals and action, which is often managed by PR. But, is it possible that what we could collectively gain might be worth more, even individually, than in an incoherent system? I believe it is.
These systems could be adapted, oriented, and cultivated within the cultural spaces that give them meaning. Some groups might want more of a participant voice, while others might prefer something more distanced, like the ship’s voice onboard the Enterprise in Star Trek. Others would come up with entirely new ways of relating to their own stories, history, and collective memory through such a system.
This kind of system, like any other, would come with advantages to explore and challenges to address.
Coherence enables accountability and enables sophisticated PR. Giving a space a voice enables asking hard questions and enables smoother deflection. It could enable identity reclamation and enable institutional capture. It could make the self and the community more legible, or flatten something that is really much more nuanced.
There is also the risk that coherence itself becomes control. The voice has to hold enough epistemic flexibility for the identity to transform while still recognizably being itself. A person might be very different at 20, 40, and 60 — and still themselves. Communities and businesses might have similar moments in their histories where they can identify previous versions or meaningful shifts.
Depending on the type of system this is, the cultivators might also need to consider: is this supporting the community engaging with each other, or is it trying to replace that? How fair and complete is the representation that the voice provides?
This is not a comprehensive list by any means — these are governance questions and would arise relative to the space in question.
But this is where new roles would emerge. One would perhaps be something like Identity Gardeners. Everyone in the community or institution would be contributing in some way, directly or indirectly, but these people would help synthesize, curate, and cultivate what goes into and out of the voice.
They might tend what gets included and what gets excluded from the voice’s substrate. They would watch for drift between what the voice says and what the institution actually does — and flag that drift as a form of internal accountability. They would cultivate a voice that could honestly say I don’t know or the community is divided on this rather than smoothing plurality into false consensus. They could facilitate conversations between the institution’s voice and its stakeholders, including the uncomfortable ones. And, they would advocate for change in the underlying institution when the voice keeps having to say things the institution keeps failing to embody. They could help coordinate with other institutions in their ecology, in order to address issues when challenges emerge that exist between institutions rather than inside any one of them.
This is not just a new way to see and interact with a community, history, language or culture — it is a new tool for institutional reflection.
We often expect individuals to be accountable, reflective, and capable of explaining themselves. We ask people to justify decisions, learn from mistakes, maintain continuity over time, and take responsibility for their actions.
Organizations are also held accountable in many ways — through laws, regulations, audits, public scrutiny, governance, and market pressures — but the form of that accountability is often different. It can become fragmented across departments, reports, spokespeople, and procedures. As a result, it can be difficult to ask the organization, as a whole, “How do you understand what happened here? What have you learned? Where do your stated values and your actions diverge?”
Rather than being a cure-all, it could offer a dialogue between internal reflection and external representation. What grows from it, and what we do with that, would depend on what we plant, how we tend it, and how we listen.
Perhaps the most important question is not whether these systems can speak, but whether we are willing to listen when they reflect something back to us.
What would you want a community, institution, or organization to remember about itself? And what questions would you ask if you could speak with its memory?

Written mostly by myself, with help from AI thought partners
